workflows_kernel_repo: https://github.com/run-llama/llama-agents (packages/llama-index-workflows — the workflow primitive was extracted out of llama_index core into its own package `workflows`; llama_index re-exports via thin shim)
execution_kernel_pattern: event-driven step-based workflow on a deterministic tick-buffer control loop (NOT asyncio.Queue/gather): each @step is wrapped in StepConfig (accepted_events, return_types, num_workers, retry_policy) derived from type-hint inspection; the runtime's _ControlLoopRunner drains a tick_buffer (list[WorkflowTick]) sequentially while running each step as its own asyncio.Task and using a heap for scheduled wakeups; pure-reducer _reduce_tick(state, tick) -> commands enables deterministic replay / time-travel

execution_kernel_source_verified.step_decorator: @step decorator → _apply_step_decorator → make_step_function → inspects function signature (inspect_signature, validate_step_signature) to derive accepted_events and return_types; attaches StepConfig as func._step_config
execution_kernel_source_verified.dispatch_validation: _validate_valid_step_message checks `type(message) not in step_config.accepted_events` before invoking step
execution_kernel_source_verified.runtime_internals: _ControlLoopRunner.tick_buffer (list), worker_tasks: set[asyncio.Task], scheduled_wakeups (heapq), TickAddEvent/TickStepResult, CommandRunWorker, _reduce_tick(state, tick) -> commands (pure reducer)
execution_kernel_source_verified.run_entry: Workflow.run() returns a WorkflowHandler (future-like, streamable); delegates to ctx._workflow_run(workflow=self, start_event=..., run_id=...)

agent_class_hierarchy_verified.AgentWorkflow: class AgentWorkflow(Workflow, PromptMixin, metaclass=AgentWorkflowMeta) — IS a Workflow subclass
agent_class_hierarchy_verified.FunctionAgent: class FunctionAgent(BaseWorkflowAgent) — NOT a Workflow subclass; it is COMPOSED inside an AgentWorkflow as a configured agent. Takes ctx: Context as a parameter to handle_tool_call_results / finalize
agent_class_hierarchy_verified.ReActAgent: class ReActAgent(BaseWorkflowAgent) — same composition pattern as FunctionAgent; differs by prompting strategy (text ReAct vs native function-calling)
agent_class_hierarchy_verified.key_takeaway: Two-level abstraction: Workflow is the runtime primitive; BaseWorkflowAgent is a CONFIG type that AgentWorkflow embeds and drives — agents are data, not workflows.

multi_agent_support.style: handoff-as-tool-call (NOT graph edges, NOT chat-room broadcast)

handoff_mechanism_source_verified.implementation: AgentWorkflow._get_handoff_tool() builds a FunctionTool.from_defaults(async_fn=handoff, description=fn_tool_prompt, return_direct=True). The handoff() async function does: `await ctx.store.set('next_agent', to_agent)` and returns a confirmation string. Because return_direct=True, the current agent's tool-execution turn ends immediately on this call.
handoff_mechanism_source_verified.agent_switch_loop: aggregate_tool_results step (the workflow's reducer-like step) reads next_agent_name = await ctx.store.get('next_agent'); if set, it does ctx.store.set('current_agent_name', next_agent_name) and clears next_agent. The next iteration picks up the new active agent.
handoff_mechanism_source_verified.constraint_enforcement: handoff() validates `to_agent in can_handoff_to.get(current_agent_name, [])` — invalid handoffs return an error string rather than raising, so the LLM can self-correct.
handoff_mechanism_source_verified.can_handoff_to_storage: stored in ctx as `{agent_name: agent_cfg.can_handoff_to}` map at workflow init

handoff_comparison_three_frameworks.llamaindex: Tool-call style. handoff() is a FunctionTool with return_direct=True; LLM emits a tool call → handler writes next_agent to Context → outer step rotates current_agent_name. Routing is data (Context state), not topology.
handoff_comparison_three_frameworks.langgraph: Topology + Command(goto=...) style. Each node returns a Command object whose goto field names the next node. Routing is encoded in the StateGraph's compiled edge map and the explicit Command return values. Typed edges, but routing is in code.
handoff_comparison_three_frameworks.openai_agents_sdk_swarm: Tool-call style (closest to llamaindex). handoffs=[Agent(...), ...] are registered; SDK generates a `transfer_to_<agent>` tool per handoff; LLM emits the tool call and the runtime swaps the active agent. Difference from llamaindex: the swap is implicit in the SDK's runner rather than via a Context flag read by a reducer step.
handoff_comparison_three_frameworks.summary: LlamaIndex and OpenAI Agents SDK share the tool-call mental model; LangGraph is the outlier with explicit Command(goto=) topology. The tool-call style is lower ceremony; the Command style gives explicit static-checkable routing.

tool_protocol: Native Python function tools (FunctionTool / @tool); tool specs auto-converted to provider function-calling schemas. MCP support is FIRST-CLASS via the dedicated llama-index-tools-mcp integration package.

mcp_integration_source_verified.client_class: class BasicMCPClient(ClientSession) — extends the MCP Python SDK's ClientSession
mcp_integration_source_verified.auth: with_oauth() classmethod for OAuth-based authentication to remote MCP servers
mcp_integration_source_verified.server_side: CLIENT-ONLY in this integration. LlamaIndex consumes MCP servers; it does NOT expose LlamaIndex workflows/agents AS an MCP server out of this package (that requires a separate MCP server wrapper).
mcp_integration_source_verified.agent_glue: McpToolSpec adapts MCP tool listings into LlamaIndex ToolSpecs, so an MCP server's tools sit alongside native FunctionTools in an agent's tool list — no special handling at the agent layer.
mcp_integration_source_verified.depth_assessment: Full client implementation of MCP spec (all three primitives — tools, prompts, resources — plus OAuth and all three transports). Notably broader than typical 'tools-only' MCP integrations seen in some other frameworks.

memory_model: Workflow Context holds short-term state for the run; ctx.store provides typed key-value state used by AgentWorkflow for current_agent_name, next_agent, scratchpad, etc. Conversational memory via ChatMemoryBuffer / VectorMemory / composable Memory blocks. Context is serializable (context_serializers.py) for durable resume — backs the durable-workflow story. Long-term/semantic memory typically delegated to a vector store via the RAG side of the library.
observability_story: Built-in instrumentation module emits typed events (LLM call, tool call, step start/end). First-party integrations with Arize Phoenix, Langfuse, LangSmith, Weights & Biases, OpenLLMetry / OpenTelemetry, Literal AI, TruLens. The deterministic tick-buffer control loop is purpose-built for replay / time-travel, which is a structural observability advantage over frameworks that just stream over asyncio.Queue.
typical_use_case: Building production RAG-augmented agents and multi-step document/data workflows where retrieval, tool use, and LLM reasoning are interleaved — e.g. document QA agents, research agents, ETL-with-LLM pipelines, SQL/text2query agents, customer support flows that need persistent context and resumability. The Workflows abstraction is also used for non-agentic structured pipelines (eval harnesses, batch ingestion).
maturity_signal: mature — 49.7k stars, MIT license, commits within the past week (2026-05-20), 300+ integration packages on LlamaHub, dedicated commercial entity (LlamaIndex Inc., LlamaCloud), llama-deploy production deployment layer, stable Workflows API since 2024 with AgentWorkflow added on top. Architectural maturity signal: the workflow primitive has been extracted out of llama_index into its own `workflows` package (llama-agents/packages/llama-index-workflows), indicating it is treated as a standalone runtime, not just a llama_index module.
one_paragraph_differentiator: Versus LangChain/LangGraph, LlamaIndex's agent layer sits on a single primitive — event-driven async Workflows — instead of LangChain's two-tier split (LCEL chains for linear, LangGraph for stateful). Where LangGraph models control flow as an explicit directed graph of nodes with Command(goto=) returns, LlamaIndex models it as Python @step functions that declare accepted Event types via type hints and run on a deterministic tick-buffer control loop; multi-agent is expressed as handoff-tool-calls between named FunctionAgent/ReActAgent instances rather than as graph nodes. The handoff mechanism (FunctionTool with return_direct=True writing next_agent to ctx.store) is mechanically closer to OpenAI Agents SDK's transfer-tool pattern than to LangGraph's typed Command edges. The other distinguishing axis is that LlamaIndex's retrieval/indexing stack is native rather than an external integration, so retrieval-tool agents are first-class. Trade-off: Workflows feel more Pythonic and less ceremonial than LangGraph, but lack LangGraph's first-class graph visualization and reducer-pattern state ergonomics — though the tick-buffer + pure-reducer architecture gives Workflows comparable replay/time-travel capability under the hood, just less surfaced in user-facing API.
known_anti_patterns: Historically conflated with pure RAG — readers assume LlamaIndex is 'just' an indexing library and miss the agent story; older docs reference deprecated agents (OpenAIAgent, legacy ReActAgent in llama_index.agent) that have been superseded by AgentWorkflow; some example notebooks remain hello-world depth (single tool, one LLM call) and don't exercise multi-agent handoff or durable Context; confusion that AgentWorkflow's `agents` are themselves Workflows — they are NOT (they are BaseWorkflowAgent configs composed inside the parent Workflow).
